vocab.definition: A naturally occurring, inorganic solid with a crystal structure and definite chemical composition
Made by Earth, not made by humans
A regular repeating pattern of atoms
A mineral property used to help identify a mineral by looking at it
The way a mineral's surface reflects light
A type of luster that is shiny and metal-like
A type of luster that can be glassy, pearly, or waxy
The powder form of a mineral, tested by rubbing across a streak plate
Unglazed porcelain used to test a mineral's streak color
A mineral's ability to resist being scratched
A scale from 1 to 10 that ranks minerals by hardness, where 1 is softest and 10 is hardest
When a mineral breaks into flat, smooth sheets
When a mineral breaks into jagged, rough pieces

mc.question: How many property tests are needed at minimum to identify a mineral?
What is the chemical formula for halite?
What is the crystal shape of halite?
Which mineral is always yellow in color?
Which mineral comes in many colors including clear, pink, brown, blue, and purple?
What type of luster does pyrite have?
Which mineral can have BOTH metallic and nonmetallic luster?
What is used to test a mineral's streak?
On the Mohs Hardness Scale, which mineral is the softest?
On the Mohs Hardness Scale, which mineral is the hardest?
What hardness can a fingernail scratch up to?
What hardness can a glass plate scratch up to?
Which of these minerals has cleavage (breaks into flat sheets)?
Which of these minerals has fracture (breaks into jagged pieces)?
Which mineral fizzes when hydrochloric acid is placed on it?
Which mineral glows under black light?
Which mineral produces a double image when you look through it?

jeopardy.question: A naturally occurring, inorganic solid with a crystal structure and definite chemical composition
Made by Earth, not made by humans
Not made of living matter
A regular repeating pattern of atoms
A mineral property used to help identify a mineral by looking at it
The way a mineral's surface reflects light
A type of luster that is shiny and metal-like
A type of luster that can be glassy, pearly, or waxy
The powder form of a mineral, tested by rubbing across a plate
Unglazed porcelain used to test a mineral's streak color
A mineral's ability to resist being scratched
A scale from 1 to 10 that ranks minerals by hardness
When a mineral breaks into flat, smooth sheets
When a mineral breaks into jagged, rough pieces
You need at least this many property tests to identify a mineral
This mineral is always yellow
This mineral can have BOTH metallic and nonmetallic luster
Talc has a hardness of 1 and this mineral has a hardness of 10
A fingernail can scratch minerals up to this hardness
A glass plate can scratch minerals up to this hardness
This mineral fizzes when hydrochloric acid is placed on it
This mineral is magnetic
This mineral glows under black light
This mineral produces a double image when you look through it
Halite has this crystal shape and is made of NaCl
Quartz can be this many colors: clear, white, pink, brown, blue, and purple
Pyrite, galena, magnetite, and graphite all have this type of luster
Mica, gypsum, calcite, galena, halite, and feldspar all have this property
